findings.title: python.flask.security.audit.secure-set-cookie.secure-set-cookie: Found a Flask cookie with insecurely configured properties.  By default the secure, httponly and samesite ar configured insecurely. cookies should be handled securely by setting `secure=True`, `httponly=True`, and `samesite='Lax'` in response.set_cookie(...). If these parameters are not properly set, your cookies are not properly protected and are at risk of being stolen by an attacker. Include the `secure=True`, `httponly=True`, `samesite='Lax'` arguments or set these to be true in the Flask configuration.

findings.title: python.flask.security.audit.secure-set-cookie.secure-set-cookie: Found a Flask cookie with insecurely configured properties.  By default the secure, httponly and samesite ar configured insecurely. cookies should be handled securely by setting `secure=True`, `httponly=True`, and `samesite='Lax'` in response.set_cookie(...). If these parameters are not properly set, your cookies are not properly protected and are at risk of being stolen by an attacker. Include the `secure=True`, `httponly=True`, `samesite='Lax'` arguments or set these to be true in the Flask configuration.

findings.title: python.django.security.injection.raw-html-format.raw-html-format: Detected user input flowing into a manually constructed HTML string. You may be accidentally bypassing secure methods of rendering HTML by manually constructing HTML and this could create a cross-site scripting vulnerability, which could let attackers steal sensitive user data. To be sure this is safe, check that the HTML is rendered safely. Otherwise, use templates (`django.shortcuts.render`) which will safely render HTML instead.
findings.description:
Semgrep rule python.django.security.injection.raw-html-format.raw-html-format detected: Detected user input flowing into a manually constructed HTML string. You may be accidentally bypassing secure methods of rendering HTML by manually constructing HTML and this could create a cross-site scripting vulnerability, which could let attackers steal sensitive user data. To be sure this is safe, check that the HTML is rendered safely. Otherwise, use templates (`django.shortcuts.render`) which will safely render HTML instead.
[Also reported by another step: Semgrep rule python.flask.security.injection.raw-html-concat.raw-html-format detected: Detected user input flowing into a manually constructed HTML string. You may be accidentally bypassing secure methods of rendering HTML by manually constructing HTML and this could create a cross-site scripting vulnerability, which could let attackers steal sensitive user data. To be sure this is safe, check that the HTML is rendered safely. Otherwise, use templates (`flask.render_template`) which will safely render HTML instead.]
findings.cwe: CWE-79: Improper Neutralization of Input During Web Page Generation ('Cross-site Scripting')
findings.tool_output: Rule: python.django.security.injection.raw-html-format.raw-html-format | Severity: WARNING | OWASP: [A07:2017 - Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) A03:2021 - Injection A05:2025 - Injection]

findings.title: python.flask.security.audit.xss.make-response-with-unknown-content.make-response-with-unknown-content: Be careful with `flask.make_response()`. If this response is rendered onto a webpage, this could create a cross-site scripting (XSS) vulnerability. `flask.make_response()` will not autoescape HTML. If you are rendering HTML, write your HTML in a template file and use `flask.render_template()` which will take care of escaping. If you are returning data from an API, consider using `flask.jsonify()`.
findings.description: Semgrep rule python.flask.security.audit.xss.make-response-with-unknown-content.make-response-with-unknown-content detected: Be careful with `flask.make_response()`. If this response is rendered onto a webpage, this could create a cross-site scripting (XSS) vulnerability. `flask.make_response()` will not autoescape HTML. If you are rendering HTML, write your HTML in a template file and use `flask.render_template()` which will take care of escaping. If you are returning data from an API, consider using `flask.jsonify()`.
findings.cwe: CWE-79: Improper Neutralization of Input During Web Page Generation ('Cross-site Scripting')
findings.tool_output: Rule: python.flask.security.audit.xss.make-response-with-unknown-content.make-response-with-unknown-content | Severity: WARNING | OWASP: [A07:2017 - Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) A03:2021 - Injection A05:2025 - Injection]

If you are returning data from an API, consider using `flask.jsonify()`.

findings.title: python.flask.security.audit.xss.make-response-with-unknown-content.make-response-with-unknown-content: Be careful with `flask.make_response()`. If this response is rendered onto a webpage, this could create a cross-site scripting (XSS) vulnerability. `flask.make_response()` will not autoescape HTML. If you are rendering HTML, write your HTML in a template file and use `flask.render_template()` which will take care of escaping. If you are returning data from an API, consider using `flask.jsonify()`.
findings.description: Semgrep rule python.flask.security.audit.xss.make-response-with-unknown-content.make-response-with-unknown-content detected: Be careful with `flask.make_response()`. If this response is rendered onto a webpage, this could create a cross-site scripting (XSS) vulnerability. `flask.make_response()` will not autoescape HTML. If you are rendering HTML, write your HTML in a template file and use `flask.render_template()` which will take care of escaping. If you are returning data from an API, consider using `flask.jsonify()`.
findings.cwe: CWE-79: Improper Neutralization of Input During Web Page Generation ('Cross-site Scripting')
findings.tool_output: Rule: python.flask.security.audit.xss.make-response-with-unknown-content.make-response-with-unknown-content | Severity: WARNING | OWASP: [A07:2017 - Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) A03:2021 - Injection A05:2025 - Injection]

findings.title: python.flask.security.insecure-deserialization.insecure-deserialization: Detected the use of an insecure deserialization library in a Flask route. These libraries are prone to code execution vulnerabilities. Ensure user data does not enter this function. To fix this, try to avoid serializing whole objects. Consider instead using a serializer such as JSON.
findings.description:
Semgrep rule python.flask.security.insecure-deserialization.insecure-deserialization detected: Detected the use of an insecure deserialization library in a Flask route. These libraries are prone to code execution vulnerabilities. Ensure user data does not enter this function. To fix this, try to avoid serializing whole objects. Consider instead using a serializer such as JSON.
[Also reported by another step: Semgrep rule python.lang.security.deserialization.pickle.avoid-pickle detected: Avoid using `pickle`, which is known to lead to code execution vulnerabilities. When unpickling, the serialized data could be manipulated to run arbitrary code. Instead, consider serializing the relevant data as JSON or a similar text-based serialization format.]

Doing so propagates current shell settings and variables, which makes it much easier for a malicious actor to execute commands. Use 'shell=False' instead.
findings.description: Semgrep rule python.lang.security.audit.subprocess-shell-true.subprocess-shell-true detected: Found 'subprocess' function 'run' with 'shell=True'. Doing so propagates current shell settings and variables, which makes it much easier for a malicious actor to execute commands. Use 'shell=False' instead.

findings.title: python.flask.security.injection.subprocess-injection.subprocess-injection: Detected user input entering a `subprocess` call unsafely. This could result in a command injection vulnerability. An attacker could use this vulnerability to execute arbitrary commands on the host, which allows them to download malware, scan sensitive data, or run any command they wish on the server. Do not let users choose the command to run. In general, prefer to use Python API versions of system commands. If you must use subprocess, use a dictionary to allowlist a set of commands.
findings.description:
Semgrep rule python.flask.security.injection.subprocess-injection.subprocess-injection detected: Detected user input entering a `subprocess` call unsafely. This could result in a command injection vulnerability. An attacker could use this vulnerability to execute arbitrary commands on the host, which allows them to download malware, scan sensitive data, or run any command they wish on the server. Do not let users choose the command to run. In general, prefer to use Python API versions of system commands. If you must use subprocess, use a dictionary to allowlist a set of commands.
[Also reported by another step: Semgrep rule python.lang.security.dangerous-subprocess-use.dangerous-subprocess-use detected: Detected subprocess function 'run' with user controlled data. A malicious actor could leverage this to perform command injection. You may consider using 'shlex.escape()'.]
[Also reported by another step: Semgrep rule python.lang.security.audit.subprocess-shell-true.subprocess-shell-true detected: Found 'subprocess' function 'run' with 'shell=True'. This is dangerous because this call will spawn the command using a shell process. Doing so propagates current shell settings and variables, which makes it much easier for a malicious actor to execute commands. Use 'shell=False' instead.]
findings.cwe: CWE-78: Improper Neutralization of Special Elements used in an OS Command ('OS Command Injection')
findings.tool_output: Rule: python.flask.security.injection.subprocess-injection.subprocess-injection | Severity: ERROR | OWASP: [A01:2017 - Injection A03:2021 - Injection A05:2025 - Injection]

findings.title: python.lang.security.use-defused-xml-parse.use-defused-xml-parse: The native Python `xml` library is vulnerable to XML External Entity (XXE) attacks. Instead  the Python documentation recommends using `defusedxml`.

Instead  the Python documentation recommends using `defusedxml`.

Instead  the Python documentation recommends using `defusedxml`.
findings.description: Semgrep rule python.lang.security.use-defused-xml-parse.use-defused-xml-parse detected: The native Python `xml` library is vulnerable to XML External Entity (XXE) attacks.  These attacks can leak confidential data and "XML bombs" can cause denial of service. Do not use this library to parse untrusted input. Instead  the Python documentation recommends using `defusedxml`.
findings.cwe: CWE-611: Improper Restriction of XML External Entity Reference
findings.tool_output: Rule: python.lang.security.use-defused-xml-parse.use-defused-xml-parse | Severity: ERROR | OWASP: [A04:2017 - XML External Entities (XXE) A05:2021 - Security Misconfiguration A02:2025 - Security Misconfiguration]

findings.title: python.flask.security.audit.xss.make-response-with-unknown-content.make-response-with-unknown-content: Be careful with `flask.make_response()`. If this response is rendered onto a webpage, this could create a cross-site scripting (XSS) vulnerability. `flask.make_response()` will not autoescape HTML. If you are rendering HTML, write your HTML in a template file and use `flask.render_template()` which will take care of escaping. If you are returning data from an API, consider using `flask.jsonify()`.
findings.description: Semgrep rule python.flask.security.audit.xss.make-response-with-unknown-content.make-response-with-unknown-content detected: Be careful with `flask.make_response()`. If this response is rendered onto a webpage, this could create a cross-site scripting (XSS) vulnerability. `flask.make_response()` will not autoescape HTML. If you are rendering HTML, write your HTML in a template file and use `flask.render_template()` which will take care of escaping. If you are returning data from an API, consider using `flask.jsonify()`.
findings.cwe: CWE-79: Improper Neutralization of Input During Web Page Generation ('Cross-site Scripting')
findings.tool_output: Rule: python.flask.security.audit.xss.make-response-with-unknown-content.make-response-with-unknown-content | Severity: WARNING | OWASP: [A07:2017 - Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) A03:2021 - Injection A05:2025 - Injection]

findings.title: python.flask.security.insecure-deserialization.insecure-deserialization: Detected the use of an insecure deserialization library in a Flask route. These libraries are prone to code execution vulnerabilities. Ensure user data does not enter this function. To fix this, try to avoid serializing whole objects. Consider instead using a serializer such as JSON.
findings.description:
Semgrep rule python.flask.security.insecure-deserialization.insecure-deserialization detected: Detected the use of an insecure deserialization library in a Flask route. These libraries are prone to code execution vulnerabilities. Ensure user data does not enter this function. To fix this, try to avoid serializing whole objects. Consider instead using a serializer such as JSON.
[Also reported by another step: Semgrep rule python.lang.security.deserialization.avoid-pyyaml-load.avoid-pyyaml-load detected: Detected a possible YAML deserialization vulnerability. `yaml.unsafe_load`, `yaml.Loader`, `yaml.CLoader`, and `yaml.UnsafeLoader` are all known to be unsafe methods of deserializing YAML. An attacker with control over the YAML input could create special YAML input that allows the attacker to run arbitrary Python code. This would allow the attacker to steal files, download and install malware, or otherwise take over the machine. Use `yaml.safe_load` or `yaml.SafeLoader` instead.]
findings.tool_output: Rule: python.flask.security.insecure-deserialization.insecure-deserialization | Severity: ERROR | OWASP: [A08:2017 - Insecure Deserialization A08:2021 - Software and Data Integrity Failures A08:2025 - Software or Data Integrity Failures]

findings.title: python.lang.security.audit.formatted-sql-query.formatted-sql-query: Detected possible formatted SQL query. Use parameterized queries instead.
findings.description:
Semgrep rule python.lang.security.audit.formatted-sql-query.formatted-sql-query detected: Detected possible formatted SQL query. Use parameterized queries instead.
[Also reported by another step: Semgrep rule python.sqlalchemy.security.sqlalchemy-execute-raw-query.sqlalchemy-execute-raw-query detected: Avoiding SQL string concatenation: untrusted input concatenated with raw SQL query can result in SQL Injection. In order to execute raw query safely, prepared statement should be used. SQLAlchemy provides TextualSQL to easily used prepared statement with named parameters. For complex SQL composition, use SQL Expression Language or Schema Definition Language. In most cases, SQLAlchemy ORM will be a better option.]
findings.cwe: CWE-89: Improper Neutralization of Special Elements used in an SQL Command ('SQL Injection')
findings.tool_output: Rule: python.lang.security.audit.formatted-sql-query.formatted-sql-query | Severity: WARNING | OWASP: [A01:2017 - Injection A03:2021 - Injection A05:2025 - Injection]

findings.title: python.flask.security.audit.xss.make-response-with-unknown-content.make-response-with-unknown-content: Be careful with `flask.make_response()`. If this response is rendered onto a webpage, this could create a cross-site scripting (XSS) vulnerability. `flask.make_response()` will not autoescape HTML. If you are rendering HTML, write your HTML in a template file and use `flask.render_template()` which will take care of escaping. If you are returning data from an API, consider using `flask.jsonify()`.

findings.title: python.flask.security.audit.secure-set-cookie.secure-set-cookie: Found a Flask cookie with insecurely configured properties.  By default the secure, httponly and samesite ar configured insecurely. cookies should be handled securely by setting `secure=True`, `httponly=True`, and `samesite='Lax'` in response.set_cookie(...). If these parameters are not properly set, your cookies are not properly protected and are at risk of being stolen by an attacker. Include the `secure=True`, `httponly=True`, `samesite='Lax'` arguments or set these to be true in the Flask configuration.
findings.description: Semgrep rule python.flask.security.audit.secure-set-cookie.secure-set-cookie detected: Found a Flask cookie with insecurely configured properties.  By default the secure, httponly and samesite ar configured insecurely. cookies should be handled securely by setting `secure=True`, `httponly=True`, and `samesite='Lax'` in response.set_cookie(...). If these parameters are not properly set, your cookies are not properly protected and are at risk of being stolen by an attacker. Include the `secure=True`, `httponly=True`, `samesite='Lax'` arguments or set these to be true in the Flask configuration.
findings.cwe: CWE-614: Sensitive Cookie in HTTPS Session Without 'Secure' Attribute
findings.tool_output: Rule: python.flask.security.audit.secure-set-cookie.secure-set-cookie | Severity: WARNING | OWASP: [A05:2021 - Security Misconfiguration A02:2025 - Security Misconfiguration]

findings.title: python.flask.security.audit.xss.make-response-with-unknown-content.make-response-with-unknown-content: Be careful with `flask.make_response()`. If this response is rendered onto a webpage, this could create a cross-site scripting (XSS) vulnerability. `flask.make_response()` will not autoescape HTML. If you are rendering HTML, write your HTML in a template file and use `flask.render_template()` which will take care of escaping. If you are returning data from an API, consider using `flask.jsonify()`.
findings.description: Semgrep rule python.flask.security.audit.xss.make-response-with-unknown-content.make-response-with-unknown-content detected: Be careful with `flask.make_response()`. If this response is rendered onto a webpage, this could create a cross-site scripting (XSS) vulnerability. `flask.make_response()` will not autoescape HTML. If you are rendering HTML, write your HTML in a template file and use `flask.render_template()` which will take care of escaping. If you are returning data from an API, consider using `flask.jsonify()`.
findings.cwe: CWE-79: Improper Neutralization of Input During Web Page Generation ('Cross-site Scripting')
findings.tool_output: Rule: python.flask.security.audit.xss.make-response-with-unknown-content.make-response-with-unknown-content | Severity: WARNING | OWASP: [A07:2017 - Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) A03:2021 - Injection A05:2025 - Injection]

findings.title: python.flask.security.audit.secure-set-cookie.secure-set-cookie: Found a Flask cookie with insecurely configured properties.  By default the secure, httponly and samesite ar configured insecurely. cookies should be handled securely by setting `secure=True`, `httponly=True`, and `samesite='Lax'` in response.set_cookie(...). If these parameters are not properly set, your cookies are not properly protected and are at risk of being stolen by an attacker. Include the `secure=True`, `httponly=True`, `samesite='Lax'` arguments or set these to be true in the Flask configuration.
findings.description: Semgrep rule python.flask.security.audit.secure-set-cookie.secure-set-cookie detected: Found a Flask cookie with insecurely configured properties. findings.title: python.flask.security.insecure-deserialization.insecure-deserialization: Detected the use of an insecure deserialization library in a Flask route. These libraries are prone to code execution vulnerabilities. Ensure user data does not enter this function. To fix this, try to avoid serializing whole objects. Consider instead using a serializer such as JSON.
findings.description:
Semgrep rule python.flask.security.insecure-deserialization.insecure-deserialization detected: Detected the use of an insecure deserialization library in a Flask route. These libraries are prone to code execution vulnerabilities. Ensure user data does not enter this function. To fix this, try to avoid serializing whole objects. Consider instead using a serializer such as JSON.
[Also reported by another step: Semgrep rule python.lang.security.deserialization.pickle.avoid-pickle detected: Avoid using `pickle`, which is known to lead to code execution vulnerabilities. When unpickling, the serialized data could be manipulated to run arbitrary code. Instead, consider serializing the relevant data as JSON or a similar text-based serialization format.]
findings.tool_output: Rule: python.flask.security.insecure-deserialization.insecure-deserialization | Severity: ERROR | OWASP: [A08:2017 - Insecure Deserialization A08:2021 - Software and Data Integrity Failures A08:2025 - Software or Data Integrity Failures]

findings.title: python.lang.security.audit.subprocess-shell-true.subprocess-shell-true: Found 'subprocess' function 'run' with 'shell=True'. This is dangerous because this call will spawn the command using a shell process. Doing so propagates current shell settings and variables, which makes it much easier for a malicious actor to execute commands. Use 'shell=False' instead.
findings.description: Semgrep rule python.lang.security.audit.subprocess-shell-true.subprocess-shell-true detected: Found 'subprocess' function 'run' with 'shell=True'. This is dangerous because this call will spawn the command using a shell process. Doing so propagates current shell settings and variables, which makes it much easier for a malicious actor to execute commands. Use 'shell=False' instead.
findings.cwe: CWE-78: Improper Neutralization of Special Elements used in an OS Command ('OS Command Injection')
findings.tool_output: Rule: python.lang.security.audit.subprocess-shell-true.subprocess-shell-true | Severity: ERROR | OWASP: [A01:2017 - Injection A03:2021 - Injection A05:2025 - Injection]

findings.title: python.flask.security.audit.xss.make-response-with-unknown-content.make-response-with-unknown-content: Be careful with `flask.make_response()`. If this response is rendered onto a webpage, this could create a cross-site scripting (XSS) vulnerability. `flask.make_response()` will not autoescape HTML. If you are rendering HTML, write your HTML in a template file and use `flask.render_template()` which will take care of escaping. If you are returning data from an API, consider using `flask.jsonify()`.
findings.description: Semgrep rule python.flask.security.audit.xss.make-response-with-unknown-content.make-response-with-unknown-content detected: Be careful with `flask.make_response()`. If this response is rendered onto a webpage, this could create a cross-site scripting (XSS) vulnerability. `flask.make_response()` will not autoescape HTML. If you are rendering HTML, write your HTML in a template file and use `flask.render_template()` which will take care of escaping. If you are returning data from an API, consider using `flask.jsonify()`.

findings.title: python.flask.security.insecure-deserialization.insecure-deserialization: Detected the use of an insecure deserialization library in a Flask route. These libraries are prone to code execution vulnerabilities. Ensure user data does not enter this function. To fix this, try to avoid serializing whole objects. Consider instead using a serializer such as JSON.
findings.description:
Semgrep rule python.flask.security.insecure-deserialization.insecure-deserialization detected: Detected the use of an insecure deserialization library in a Flask route. These libraries are prone to code execution vulnerabilities. Ensure user data does not enter this function. To fix this, try to avoid serializing whole objects. Consider instead using a serializer such as JSON.
[Also reported by another step: Semgrep rule python.lang.security.deserialization.avoid-pyyaml-load.avoid-pyyaml-load detected: Detected a possible YAML deserialization vulnerability. `yaml.unsafe_load`, `yaml.Loader`, `yaml.CLoader`, and `yaml.UnsafeLoader` are all known to be unsafe methods of deserializing YAML. An attacker with control over the YAML input could create special YAML input that allows the attacker to run arbitrary Python code. This would allow the attacker to steal files, download and install malware, or otherwise take over the machine. Use `yaml.safe_load` or `yaml.SafeLoader` instead.]
findings.tool_output: Rule: python.flask.security.insecure-deserialization.insecure-deserialization | Severity: ERROR | OWASP: [A08:2017 - Insecure Deserialization A08:2021 - Software and Data Integrity Failures A08:2025 - Software or Data Integrity Failures]

findings.title: python.django.security.injection.tainted-sql-string.tainted-sql-string: Detected user input used to manually construct a SQL string. This is usually bad practice because manual construction could accidentally result in a SQL injection. An attacker could use a SQL injection to steal or modify contents of the database. Instead, use a parameterized query which is available by default in most database engines. Alternatively, consider using the Django object-relational mappers (ORM) instead of raw SQL queries.
findings.description: Semgrep rule python.django.security.injection.tainted-sql-string.tainted-sql-string detected: Detected user input used to manually construct a SQL string. This is usually bad practice because manual construction could accidentally result in a SQL injection. An attacker could use a SQL injection to steal or modify contents of the database. Instead, use a parameterized query which is available by default in most database engines. Alternatively, consider using the Django object-relational mappers (ORM) instead of raw SQL queries.

findings.title: python.django.security.injection.raw-html-format.raw-html-format: Detected user input flowing into a manually constructed HTML string. You may be accidentally bypassing secure methods of rendering HTML by manually constructing HTML and this could create a cross-site scripting vulnerability, which could let attackers steal sensitive user data. To be sure this is safe, check that the HTML is rendered safely. Otherwise, use templates (`django.shortcuts.render`) which will safely render HTML instead.

To be sure this is safe, check that the HTML is rendered safely. Otherwise, use templates (`django.shortcuts.render`) which will safely render HTML instead.

findings.title: python.django.security.injection.request-data-write.request-data-write: Found user-controlled request data passed into '.write(...)'. This could be dangerous if a malicious actor is able to control data into sensitive files. For example, a malicious actor could force rolling of critical log files, or cause a denial-of-service by using up available disk space. Instead, ensure that request data is properly escaped or sanitized.

findings.title: python.flask.security.audit.xss.make-response-with-unknown-content.make-response-with-unknown-content: Be careful with `flask.make_response()`. If this response is rendered onto a webpage, this could create a cross-site scripting (XSS) vulnerability. `flask.make_response()` will not autoescape HTML. If you are rendering HTML, write your HTML in a template file and use `flask.render_template()` which will take care of escaping. If you are returning data from an API, consider using `flask.jsonify()`.
findings.description: Semgrep rule python.flask.security.audit.xss.make-response-with-unknown-content.make-response-with-unknown-content detected: Be careful with `flask.make_response()`. If this response is rendered onto a webpage, this could create a cross-site scripting (XSS) vulnerability. `flask.make_response()` will not autoescape HTML. If you are rendering HTML, write your HTML in a template file and use `flask.render_template()` which will take care of escaping. If you are returning data from an API, consider using `flask.jsonify()`.
findings.cwe: CWE-79: Improper Neutralization of Input During Web Page Generation ('Cross-site Scripting')
findings.tool_output: Rule: python.flask.security.audit.xss.make-response-with-unknown-content.make-response-with-unknown-content | Severity: WARNING | OWASP: [A07:2017 - Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) A03:2021 - Injection A05:2025 - Injection]

findings.title: python.flask.security.audit.secure-set-cookie.secure-set-cookie: Found a Flask cookie with insecurely configured properties.  By default the secure, httponly and samesite ar configured insecurely. cookies should be handled securely by setting `secure=True`, `httponly=True`, and `samesite='Lax'` in response.set_cookie(...). If these parameters are not properly set, your cookies are not properly protected and are at risk of being stolen by an attacker. Include the `secure=True`, `httponly=True`, `samesite='Lax'` arguments or set these to be true in the Flask configuration.
findings.description: Semgrep rule python.flask.security.audit.secure-set-cookie.secure-set-cookie detected: Found a Flask cookie with insecurely configured properties.  By default the secure, httponly and samesite ar configured insecurely. cookies should be handled securely by setting `secure=True`, `httponly=True`, and `samesite='Lax'` in response.set_cookie(...). If these parameters are not properly set, your cookies are not properly protected and are at risk of being stolen by an attacker. Include the `secure=True`, `httponly=True`, `samesite='Lax'` arguments or set these to be true in the Flask configuration.
findings.cwe: CWE-614: Sensitive Cookie in HTTPS Session Without 'Secure' Attribute
findings.tool_output: Rule: python.flask.security.audit.secure-set-cookie.secure-set-cookie | Severity: WARNING | OWASP: [A05:2021 - Security Misconfiguration A02:2025 - Security Misconfiguration]

These libraries are prone to code execution vulnerabilities. Ensure user data does not enter this function. To fix this, try to avoid serializing whole objects. Consider instead using a serializer such as JSON.
findings.description:
Semgrep rule python.flask.security.insecure-deserialization.insecure-deserialization detected: Detected the use of an insecure deserialization library in a Flask route. These libraries are prone to code execution vulnerabilities. Ensure user data does not enter this function. To fix this, try to avoid serializing whole objects. Consider instead using a serializer such as JSON.
[Also reported by another step: Semgrep rule python.lang.security.deserialization.pickle.avoid-pickle detected: Avoid using `pickle`, which is known to lead to code execution vulnerabilities. findings.title: python.lang.security.audit.subprocess-shell-true.subprocess-shell-true: Found 'subprocess' function 'run' with 'shell=True'. This is dangerous because this call will spawn the command using a shell process. Doing so propagates current shell settings and variables, which makes it much easier for a malicious actor to execute commands. Use 'shell=False' instead.
findings.description: Semgrep rule python.lang.security.audit.subprocess-shell-true.subprocess-shell-true detected: Found 'subprocess' function 'run' with 'shell=True'. This is dangerous because this call will spawn the command using a shell process. Doing so propagates current shell settings and variables, which makes it much easier for a malicious actor to execute commands. Use 'shell=False' instead.
findings.cwe: CWE-78: Improper Neutralization of Special Elements used in an OS Command ('OS Command Injection')

An attacker with control over the YAML input could create special YAML input that allows the attacker to run arbitrary Python code. This would allow the attacker to steal files, download and install malware, or otherwise take over the machine. Use `yaml.safe_load` or `yaml.SafeLoader` instead.]

findings.title: python.django.security.injection.raw-html-format.raw-html-format: Detected user input flowing into a manually constructed HTML string. You may be accidentally bypassing secure methods of rendering HTML by manually constructing HTML and this could create a cross-site scripting vulnerability, which could let attackers steal sensitive user data. To be sure this is safe, check that the HTML is rendered safely. Otherwise, use templates (`django.shortcuts.render`) which will safely render HTML instead.
findings.description:
Semgrep rule python.django.security.injection.raw-html-format.raw-html-format detected: Detected user input flowing into a manually constructed HTML string. You may be accidentally bypassing secure methods of rendering HTML by manually constructing HTML and this could create a cross-site scripting vulnerability, which could let attackers steal sensitive user data. To be sure this is safe, check that the HTML is rendered safely. Otherwise, use templates (`django.shortcuts.render`) which will safely render HTML instead.
[Also reported by another step: Semgrep rule python.flask.security.injection.raw-html-concat.raw-html-format detected: Detected user input flowing into a manually constructed HTML string. You may be accidentally bypassing secure methods of rendering HTML by manually constructing HTML and this could create a cross-site scripting vulnerability, which could let attackers steal sensitive user data. To be sure this is safe, check that the HTML is rendered safely. Otherwise, use templates (`flask.render_template`) which will safely render HTML instead.]

findings.title: python.flask.security.audit.xss.make-response-with-unknown-content.make-response-with-unknown-content: Be careful with `flask.make_response()`. If this response is rendered onto a webpage, this could create a cross-site scripting (XSS) vulnerability. `flask.make_response()` will not autoescape HTML. If you are rendering HTML, write your HTML in a template file and use `flask.render_template()` which will take care of escaping. If you are returning data from an API, consider using `flask.jsonify()`.
findings.description: Semgrep rule python.flask.security.audit.xss.make-response-with-unknown-content.make-response-with-unknown-content detected: Be careful with `flask.make_response()`. If this response is rendered onto a webpage, this could create a cross-site scripting (XSS) vulnerability. `flask.make_response()` will not autoescape HTML. If you are rendering HTML, write your HTML in a template file and use `flask.render_template()` which will take care of escaping. If you are returning data from an API, consider using `flask.jsonify()`.
findings.cwe: CWE-79: Improper Neutralization of Input During Web Page Generation ('Cross-site Scripting')
findings.tool_output: Rule: python.flask.security.audit.xss.make-response-with-unknown-content.make-response-with-unknown-content | Severity: WARNING | OWASP: [A07:2017 - Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) A03:2021 - Injection A05:2025 - Injection]

findings.title: python.flask.security.audit.secure-set-cookie.secure-set-cookie: Found a Flask cookie with insecurely configured properties.  By default the secure, httponly and samesite ar configured insecurely. cookies should be handled securely by setting `secure=True`, `httponly=True`, and `samesite='Lax'` in response.set_cookie(...). If these parameters are not properly set, your cookies are not properly protected and are at risk of being stolen by an attacker. Include the `secure=True`, `httponly=True`, `samesite='Lax'` arguments or set these to be true in the Flask configuration.
findings.description: Semgrep rule python.flask.security.audit.secure-set-cookie.secure-set-cookie detected: Found a Flask cookie with insecurely configured properties.  By default the secure, httponly and samesite ar configured insecurely. cookies should be handled securely by setting `secure=True`, `httponly=True`, and `samesite='Lax'` in response.set_cookie(...). If these parameters are not properly set, your cookies are not properly protected and are at risk of being stolen by an attacker. Include the `secure=True`, `httponly=True`, `samesite='Lax'` arguments or set these to be true in the Flask configuration.
findings.cwe: CWE-614: Sensitive Cookie in HTTPS Session Without 'Secure' Attribute
findings.tool_output: Rule: python.flask.security.audit.secure-set-cookie.secure-set-cookie | Severity: WARNING | OWASP: [A05:2021 - Security Misconfiguration A02:2025 - Security Misconfiguration]

findings.title: python.flask.security.insecure-deserialization.insecure-deserialization: Detected the use of an insecure deserialization library in a Flask route. These libraries are prone to code execution vulnerabilities. Ensure user data does not enter this function. To fix this, try to avoid serializing whole objects. Consider instead using a serializer such as JSON.
findings.description:
Semgrep rule python.flask.security.insecure-deserialization.insecure-deserialization detected: Detected the use of an insecure deserialization library in a Flask route. These libraries are prone to code execution vulnerabilities. Ensure user data does not enter this function. To fix this, try to avoid serializing whole objects. Consider instead using a serializer such as JSON.
[Also reported by another step: Semgrep rule python.lang.security.deserialization.pickle.avoid-pickle detected: Avoid using `pickle`, which is known to lead to code execution vulnerabilities. When unpickling, the serialized data could be manipulated to run arbitrary code. Instead, consider serializing the relevant data as JSON or a similar text-based serialization format.]
findings.tool_output: Rule: python.flask.security.insecure-deserialization.insecure-deserialization | Severity: ERROR | OWASP: [A08:2017 - Insecure Deserialization A08:2021 - Software and Data Integrity Failures A08:2025 - Software or Data Integrity Failures]

findings.title: python.lang.security.audit.subprocess-shell-true.subprocess-shell-true: Found 'subprocess' function 'run' with 'shell=True'. This is dangerous because this call will spawn the command using a shell process. Doing so propagates current shell settings and variables, which makes it much easier for a malicious actor to execute commands. Use 'shell=False' instead.
findings.description: Semgrep rule python.lang.security.audit.subprocess-shell-true.subprocess-shell-true detected: Found 'subprocess' function 'run' with 'shell=True'. This is dangerous because this call will spawn the command using a shell process. Doing so propagates current shell settings and variables, which makes it much easier for a malicious actor to execute commands. Use 'shell=False' instead.
findings.cwe: CWE-78: Improper Neutralization of Special Elements used in an OS Command ('OS Command Injection')

An attacker with control over the YAML input could create special YAML input that allows the attacker to run arbitrary Python code. findings.description:
Semgrep rule python.flask.security.insecure-deserialization.insecure-deserialization detected: Detected the use of an insecure deserialization library in a Flask route. These libraries are prone to code execution vulnerabilities. Ensure user data does not enter this function. To fix this, try to avoid serializing whole objects. Consider instead using a serializer such as JSON.
[Also reported by another step: Semgrep rule python.lang.security.deserialization.avoid-pyyaml-load.avoid-pyyaml-load detected: Detected a possible YAML deserialization vulnerability. `yaml.unsafe_load`, `yaml.Loader`, `yaml.CLoader`, and `yaml.UnsafeLoader` are all known to be unsafe methods of deserializing YAML. An attacker with control over the YAML input could create special YAML input that allows the attacker to run arbitrary Python code. This would allow the attacker to steal files, download and install malware, or otherwise take over the machine. Use `yaml.safe_load` or `yaml.SafeLoader` instead.]

findings.title: python.django.security.injection.raw-html-format.raw-html-format: Detected user input flowing into a manually constructed HTML string. You may be accidentally bypassing secure methods of rendering HTML by manually constructing HTML and this could create a cross-site scripting vulnerability, which could let attackers steal sensitive user data. To be sure this is safe, check that the HTML is rendered safely. Otherwise, use templates (`django.shortcuts.render`) which will safely render HTML instead.
findings.description:

findings.title: python.flask.security.audit.xss.make-response-with-unknown-content.make-response-with-unknown-content: Be careful with `flask.make_response()`. If this response is rendered onto a webpage, this could create a cross-site scripting (XSS) vulnerability. `flask.make_response()` will not autoescape HTML. If you are rendering HTML, write your HTML in a template file and use `flask.render_template()` which will take care of escaping. If you are returning data from an API, consider using `flask.jsonify()`.
findings.description: Semgrep rule python.flask.security.audit.xss.make-response-with-unknown-content.make-response-with-unknown-content detected: Be careful with `flask.make_response()`. If this response is rendered onto a webpage, this could create a cross-site scripting (XSS) vulnerability. `flask.make_response()` will not autoescape HTML. If you are rendering HTML, write your HTML in a template file and use `flask.render_template()` which will take care of escaping. If you are returning data from an API, consider using `flask.jsonify()`.
findings.cwe: CWE-79: Improper Neutralization of Input During Web Page Generation ('Cross-site Scripting')
findings.tool_output: Rule: python.flask.security.audit.xss.make-response-with-unknown-content.make-response-with-unknown-content | Severity: WARNING | OWASP: [A07:2017 - Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) A03:2021 - Injection A05:2025 - Injection]

findings.title: python.flask.security.audit.secure-set-cookie.secure-set-cookie: Found a Flask cookie with insecurely configured properties.  By default the secure, httponly and samesite ar configured insecurely. cookies should be handled securely by setting `secure=True`, `httponly=True`, and `samesite='Lax'` in response.set_cookie(...). If these parameters are not properly set, your cookies are not properly protected and are at risk of being stolen by an attacker. Include the `secure=True`, `httponly=True`, `samesite='Lax'` arguments or set these to be true in the Flask configuration.
findings.description: Semgrep rule python.flask.security.audit.secure-set-cookie.secure-set-cookie detected: Found a Flask cookie with insecurely configured properties.  By default the secure, httponly and samesite ar configured insecurely. cookies should be handled securely by setting `secure=True`, `httponly=True`, and `samesite='Lax'` in response.set_cookie(...). If these parameters are not properly set, your cookies are not properly protected and are at risk of being stolen by an attacker. Include the `secure=True`, `httponly=True`, `samesite='Lax'` arguments or set these to be true in the Flask configuration.
findings.cwe: CWE-614: Sensitive Cookie in HTTPS Session Without 'Secure' Attribute
findings.tool_output: Rule: python.flask.security.audit.secure-set-cookie.secure-set-cookie | Severity: WARNING | OWASP: [A05:2021 - Security Misconfiguration A02:2025 - Security Misconfiguration]

If you are rendering HTML, write your HTML in a template file and use `flask.render_template()` which will take care of escaping. If you are returning data from an API, consider using `flask.jsonify()`.
findings.description: Semgrep rule python.flask.security.audit.xss.make-response-with-unknown-content.make-response-with-unknown-content detected: Be careful with `flask.make_response()`. If this response is rendered onto a webpage, this could create a cross-site scripting (XSS) vulnerability. `flask.make_response()` will not autoescape HTML. If you are rendering HTML, write your HTML in a template file and use `flask.render_template()` which will take care of escaping. If you are returning data from an API, consider using `flask.jsonify()`.
findings.cwe: CWE-79: Improper Neutralization of Input During Web Page Generation ('Cross-site Scripting')
findings.tool_output: Rule: python.flask.security.audit.xss.make-response-with-unknown-content.make-response-with-unknown-content | Severity: WARNING | OWASP: [A07:2017 - Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) A03:2021 - Injection A05:2025 - Injection]

findings.title: python.flask.security.audit.secure-set-cookie.secure-set-cookie: Found a Flask cookie with insecurely configured properties.  By default the secure, httponly and samesite ar configured insecurely. cookies should be handled securely by setting `secure=True`, `httponly=True`, and `samesite='Lax'` in response.set_cookie(...). If these parameters are not properly set, your cookies are not properly protected and are at risk of being stolen by an attacker. Include the `secure=True`, `httponly=True`, `samesite='Lax'` arguments or set these to be true in the Flask configuration.
findings.description: Semgrep rule python.flask.security.audit.secure-set-cookie.secure-set-cookie detected: Found a Flask cookie with insecurely configured properties.  By default the secure, httponly and samesite ar configured insecurely. cookies should be handled securely by setting `secure=True`, `httponly=True`, and `samesite='Lax'` in response.set_cookie(...). If these parameters are not properly set, your cookies are not properly protected and are at risk of being stolen by an attacker. Include the `secure=True`, `httponly=True`, `samesite='Lax'` arguments or set these to be true in the Flask configuration.
findings.cwe: CWE-614: Sensitive Cookie in HTTPS Session Without 'Secure' Attribute
findings.tool_output: Rule: python.flask.security.audit.secure-set-cookie.secure-set-cookie | Severity: WARNING | OWASP: [A05:2021 - Security Misconfiguration A02:2025 - Security Misconfiguration]

findings.title: python.django.security.audit.avoid-insecure-deserialization.avoid-insecure-deserialization: Avoid using insecure deserialization library, backed by `pickle`, `_pickle`, `cpickle`, `dill`, `shelve`, or `yaml`, which are known to lead to remote code execution vulnerabilities.
findings.description:
Semgrep rule python.django.security.audit.avoid-insecure-deserialization.avoid-insecure-deserialization detected: Avoid using insecure deserialization library, backed by `pickle`, `_pickle`, `cpickle`, `dill`, `shelve`, or `yaml`, which are known to lead to remote code execution vulnerabilities.
[Also reported by another step: Semgrep rule python.flask.security.insecure-deserialization.insecure-deserialization detected: Detected the use of an insecure deserialization library in a Flask route. These libraries are prone to code execution vulnerabilities. Ensure user data does not enter this function. To fix this, try to avoid serializing whole objects. Consider instead using a serializer such as JSON.]
[Also reported by another step: Semgrep rule python.lang.security.deserialization.pickle.avoid-pickle detected: Avoid using `pickle`, which is known to lead to code execution vulnerabilities. When unpickling, the serialized data could be manipulated to run arbitrary code. Instead, consider serializing the relevant data as JSON or a similar text-based serialization format.]
findings.tool_output: Rule: python.django.security.audit.avoid-insecure-deserialization.avoid-insecure-deserialization | Severity: ERROR | OWASP: [A08:2017 - Insecure Deserialization A08:2021 - Software and Data Integrity Failures A08:2025 - Software or Data Integrity Failures]

findings.title: python.lang.security.audit.subprocess-shell-true.subprocess-shell-true: Found 'subprocess' function 'run' with 'shell=True'. This is dangerous because this call will spawn the command using a shell process. Doing so propagates current shell settings and variables, which makes it much easier for a malicious actor to execute commands. Use 'shell=False' instead.
findings.description: Semgrep rule python.lang.security.audit.subprocess-shell-true.subprocess-shell-true detected: Found 'subprocess' function 'run' with 'shell=True'. This is dangerous because this call will spawn the command using a shell process. Doing so propagates current shell settings and variables, which makes it much easier for a malicious actor to execute commands. Use 'shell=False' instead.
findings.cwe: CWE-78: Improper Neutralization of Special Elements used in an OS Command ('OS Command Injection')
findings.tool_output: Rule: python.lang.security.audit.subprocess-shell-true.subprocess-shell-true | Severity: ERROR | OWASP: [A01:2017 - Injection A03:2021 - Injection A05:2025 - Injection]

If this response is rendered onto a webpage, this could create a cross-site scripting (XSS) vulnerability. `flask.make_response()` will not autoescape HTML. If you are rendering HTML, write your HTML in a template file and use `flask.render_template()` which will take care of escaping. If you are returning data from an API, consider using `flask.jsonify()`.
findings.description: Semgrep rule python.flask.security.audit.xss.make-response-with-unknown-content.make-response-with-unknown-content detected: Be careful with `flask.make_response()`. If this response is rendered onto a webpage, this could create a cross-site scripting (XSS) vulnerability. `flask.make_response()` will not autoescape HTML. If you are rendering HTML, write your HTML in a template file and use `flask.render_template()` which will take care of escaping. If you are returning data from an API, consider using `flask.jsonify()`.
findings.cwe: CWE-79: Improper Neutralization of Input During Web Page Generation ('Cross-site Scripting')
findings.tool_output: Rule: python.flask.security.audit.xss.make-response-with-unknown-content.make-response-with-unknown-content | Severity: WARNING | OWASP: [A07:2017 - Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) A03:2021 - Injection A05:2025 - Injection]

findings.title: python.flask.security.audit.secure-set-cookie.secure-set-cookie: Found a Flask cookie with insecurely configured properties.  By default the secure, httponly and samesite ar configured insecurely. cookies should be handled securely by setting `secure=True`, `httponly=True`, and `samesite='Lax'` in response.set_cookie(...). If these parameters are not properly set, your cookies are not properly protected and are at risk of being stolen by an attacker. Include the `secure=True`, `httponly=True`, `samesite='Lax'` arguments or set these to be true in the Flask configuration.
findings.description: Semgrep rule python.flask.security.audit.secure-set-cookie.secure-set-cookie detected: Found a Flask cookie with insecurely configured properties.  By default the secure, httponly and samesite ar configured insecurely. cookies should be handled securely by setting `secure=True`, `httponly=True`, and `samesite='Lax'` in response.set_cookie(...). If these parameters are not properly set, your cookies are not properly protected and are at risk of being stolen by an attacker. Include the `secure=True`, `httponly=True`, `samesite='Lax'` arguments or set these to be true in the Flask configuration.
findings.cwe: CWE-614: Sensitive Cookie in HTTPS Session Without 'Secure' Attribute
findings.tool_output: Rule: python.flask.security.audit.secure-set-cookie.secure-set-cookie | Severity: WARNING | OWASP: [A05:2021 - Security Misconfiguration A02:2025 - Security Misconfiguration]

findings.title: python.flask.security.insecure-deserialization.insecure-deserialization: Detected the use of an insecure deserialization library in a Flask route. These libraries are prone to code execution vulnerabilities. Ensure user data does not enter this function. To fix this, try to avoid serializing whole objects. Consider instead using a serializer such as JSON.
findings.description:
Semgrep rule python.flask.security.insecure-deserialization.insecure-deserialization detected: Detected the use of an insecure deserialization library in a Flask route. These libraries are prone to code execution vulnerabilities. Ensure user data does not enter this function. To fix this, try to avoid serializing whole objects. Consider instead using a serializer such as JSON.
[Also reported by another step: Semgrep rule python.lang.security.deserialization.pickle.avoid-pickle detected: Avoid using `pickle`, which is known to lead to code execution vulnerabilities. When unpickling, the serialized data could be manipulated to run arbitrary code. Instead, consider serializing the relevant data as JSON or a similar text-based serialization format.]
findings.tool_output: Rule: python.flask.security.insecure-deserialization.insecure-deserialization | Severity: ERROR | OWASP: [A08:2017 - Insecure Deserialization A08:2021 - Software and Data Integrity Failures A08:2025 - Software or Data Integrity Failures]

An attacker with control over the YAML input could create special YAML input that allows the attacker to run arbitrary Python code. This would allow the attacker to steal files, download and install malware, or otherwise take over the machine. Use `yaml.safe_load` or `yaml.SafeLoader` instead.]

findings.title: python.lang.security.use-defused-xml-parse.use-defused-xml-parse: The native Python `xml` library is vulnerable to XML External Entity (XXE) attacks.  These attacks can leak confidential data and "XML bombs" can cause denial of service. Do not use this library to parse untrusted input. Instead  the Python documentation recommends using `defusedxml`.

Instead  the Python documentation recommends using `defusedxml`.

findings.title: python.flask.security.audit.xss.make-response-with-unknown-content.make-response-with-unknown-content: Be careful with `flask.make_response()`. If this response is rendered onto a webpage, this could create a cross-site scripting (XSS) vulnerability. `flask.make_response()` will not autoescape HTML. If you are rendering HTML, write your HTML in a template file and use `flask.render_template()` which will take care of escaping. If you are returning data from an API, consider using `flask.jsonify()`.
findings.description: Semgrep rule python.flask.security.audit.xss.make-response-with-unknown-content.make-response-with-unknown-content detected: Be careful with `flask.make_response()`. If this response is rendered onto a webpage, this could create a cross-site scripting (XSS) vulnerability. `flask.make_response()` will not autoescape HTML. If you are rendering HTML, write your HTML in a template file and use `flask.render_template()` which will take care of escaping. If you are returning data from an API, consider using `flask.jsonify()`.
findings.cwe: CWE-79: Improper Neutralization of Input During Web Page Generation ('Cross-site Scripting')
findings.tool_output: Rule: python.flask.security.audit.xss.make-response-with-unknown-content.make-response-with-unknown-content | Severity: WARNING | OWASP: [A07:2017 - Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) A03:2021 - Injection A05:2025 - Injection]

findings.title: python.flask.security.audit.secure-set-cookie.secure-set-cookie: Found a Flask cookie with insecurely configured properties.  By default the secure, httponly and samesite ar configured insecurely. cookies should be handled securely by setting `secure=True`, `httponly=True`, and `samesite='Lax'` in response.set_cookie(...). If these parameters are not properly set, your cookies are not properly protected and are at risk of being stolen by an attacker. Include the `secure=True`, `httponly=True`, `samesite='Lax'` arguments or set these to be true in the Flask configuration.
findings.description: Semgrep rule python.flask.security.audit.secure-set-cookie.secure-set-cookie detected: Found a Flask cookie with insecurely configured properties.  By default the secure, httponly and samesite ar configured insecurely. cookies should be handled securely by setting `secure=True`, `httponly=True`, and `samesite='Lax'` in response.set_cookie(...). If these parameters are not properly set, your cookies are not properly protected and are at risk of being stolen by an attacker. Include the `secure=True`, `httponly=True`, `samesite='Lax'` arguments or set these to be true in the Flask configuration.
findings.cwe: CWE-614: Sensitive Cookie in HTTPS Session Without 'Secure' Attribute
findings.tool_output: Rule: python.flask.security.audit.secure-set-cookie.secure-set-cookie | Severity: WARNING | OWASP: [A05:2021 - Security Misconfiguration A02:2025 - Security Misconfiguration]

findings.title: python.lang.security.audit.eval-detected.eval-detected: Detected the use of eval(). eval() can be dangerous if used to evaluate dynamic content. If this content can be input from outside the program, this may be a code injection vulnerability. Ensure evaluated content is not definable by external sources.
findings.description: Semgrep rule python.lang.security.audit.eval-detected.eval-detected detected: Detected the use of eval(). eval() can be dangerous if used to evaluate dynamic content. findings.title: python.flask.security.insecure-deserialization.insecure-deserialization: Detected the use of an insecure deserialization library in a Flask route. These libraries are prone to code execution vulnerabilities. Ensure user data does not enter this function. To fix this, try to avoid serializing whole objects. Consider instead using a serializer such as JSON.
findings.description:
Semgrep rule python.flask.security.insecure-deserialization.insecure-deserialization detected: Detected the use of an insecure deserialization library in a Flask route. These libraries are prone to code execution vulnerabilities. Ensure user data does not enter this function. To fix this, try to avoid serializing whole objects. Consider instead using a serializer such as JSON.
[Also reported by another step: Semgrep rule python.lang.security.deserialization.pickle.avoid-pickle detected: Avoid using `pickle`, which is known to lead to code execution vulnerabilities. When unpickling, the serialized data could be manipulated to run arbitrary code. Instead, consider serializing the relevant data as JSON or a similar text-based serialization format.]
findings.tool_output: Rule: python.flask.security.insecure-deserialization.insecure-deserialization | Severity: ERROR | OWASP: [A08:2017 - Insecure Deserialization A08:2021 - Software and Data Integrity Failures A08:2025 - Software or Data Integrity Failures]

findings.title: python.lang.security.audit.subprocess-shell-true.subprocess-shell-true: Found 'subprocess' function 'run' with 'shell=True'. This is dangerous because this call will spawn the command using a shell process. Doing so propagates current shell settings and variables, which makes it much easier for a malicious actor to execute commands. Use 'shell=False' instead.
findings.description: Semgrep rule python.lang.security.audit.subprocess-shell-true.subprocess-shell-true detected: Found 'subprocess' function 'run' with 'shell=True'. This is dangerous because this call will spawn the command using a shell process. Doing so propagates current shell settings and variables, which makes it much easier for a malicious actor to execute commands. Use 'shell=False' instead.
findings.cwe: CWE-78: Improper Neutralization of Special Elements used in an OS Command ('OS Command Injection')
findings.tool_output: Rule: python.lang.security.audit.subprocess-shell-true.subprocess-shell-true | Severity: ERROR | OWASP: [A01:2017 - Injection A03:2021 - Injection A05:2025 - Injection]

findings.title: python.django.security.injection.raw-html-format.raw-html-format: Detected user input flowing into a manually constructed HTML string. You may be accidentally bypassing secure methods of rendering HTML by manually constructing HTML and this could create a cross-site scripting vulnerability, which could let attackers steal sensitive user data. To be sure this is safe, check that the HTML is rendered safely. Otherwise, use templates (`django.shortcuts.render`) which will safely render HTML instead.

findings.title: python.flask.security.audit.xss.make-response-with-unknown-content.make-response-with-unknown-content: Be careful with `flask.make_response()`. If this response is rendered onto a webpage, this could create a cross-site scripting (XSS) vulnerability. `flask.make_response()` will not autoescape HTML. If you are rendering HTML, write your HTML in a template file and use `flask.render_template()` which will take care of escaping. If you are returning data from an API, consider using `flask.jsonify()`.
findings.description: Semgrep rule python.flask.security.audit.xss.make-response-with-unknown-content.make-response-with-unknown-content detected: Be careful with `flask.make_response()`. If this response is rendered onto a webpage, this could create a cross-site scripting (XSS) vulnerability. `flask.make_response()` will not autoescape HTML. If you are rendering HTML, write your HTML in a template file and use `flask.render_template()` which will take care of escaping. If you are returning data from an API, consider using `flask.jsonify()`.
findings.cwe: CWE-79: Improper Neutralization of Input During Web Page Generation ('Cross-site Scripting')
findings.tool_output: Rule: python.flask.security.audit.xss.make-response-with-unknown-content.make-response-with-unknown-content | Severity: WARNING | OWASP: [A07:2017 - Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) A03:2021 - Injection A05:2025 - Injection]

If you are rendering HTML, write your HTML in a template file and use `flask.render_template()` which will take care of escaping. If you are returning data from an API, consider using `flask.jsonify()`.
findings.description: Semgrep rule python.flask.security.audit.xss.make-response-with-unknown-content.make-response-with-unknown-content detected: Be careful with `flask.make_response()`. If this response is rendered onto a webpage, this could create a cross-site scripting (XSS) vulnerability. `flask.make_response()` will not autoescape HTML. If you are rendering HTML, write your HTML in a template file and use `flask.render_template()` which will take care of escaping. If you are returning data from an API, consider using `flask.jsonify()`.
findings.cwe: CWE-79: Improper Neutralization of Input During Web Page Generation ('Cross-site Scripting')
findings.tool_output: Rule: python.flask.security.audit.xss.make-response-with-unknown-content.make-response-with-unknown-content | Severity: WARNING | OWASP: [A07:2017 - Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) A03:2021 - Injection A05:2025 - Injection]

findings.title: python.flask.security.audit.secure-set-cookie.secure-set-cookie: Found a Flask cookie with insecurely configured properties.  By default the secure, httponly and samesite ar configured insecurely. cookies should be handled securely by setting `secure=True`, `httponly=True`, and `samesite='Lax'` in response.set_cookie(...). If these parameters are not properly set, your cookies are not properly protected and are at risk of being stolen by an attacker. Include the `secure=True`, `httponly=True`, `samesite='Lax'` arguments or set these to be true in the Flask configuration.
findings.description: Semgrep rule python.flask.security.audit.secure-set-cookie.secure-set-cookie detected: Found a Flask cookie with insecurely configured properties.  By default the secure, httponly and samesite ar configured insecurely. cookies should be handled securely by setting `secure=True`, `httponly=True`, and `samesite='Lax'` in response.set_cookie(...). If these parameters are not properly set, your cookies are not properly protected and are at risk of being stolen by an attacker. Include the `secure=True`, `httponly=True`, `samesite='Lax'` arguments or set these to be true in the Flask configuration.
findings.cwe: CWE-614: Sensitive Cookie in HTTPS Session Without 'Secure' Attribute
findings.tool_output: Rule: python.flask.security.audit.secure-set-cookie.secure-set-cookie | Severity: WARNING | OWASP: [A05:2021 - Security Misconfiguration A02:2025 - Security Misconfiguration]

findings.title: python.flask.security.insecure-deserialization.insecure-deserialization: Detected the use of an insecure deserialization library in a Flask route. These libraries are prone to code execution vulnerabilities. Ensure user data does not enter this function. To fix this, try to avoid serializing whole objects. Consider instead using a serializer such as JSON.
findings.description:
Semgrep rule python.flask.security.insecure-deserialization.insecure-deserialization detected: Detected the use of an insecure deserialization library in a Flask route. These libraries are prone to code execution vulnerabilities. Ensure user data does not enter this function. To fix this, try to avoid serializing whole objects. Consider instead using a serializer such as JSON.
[Also reported by another step: Semgrep rule python.lang.security.deserialization.pickle.avoid-pickle detected: Avoid using `pickle`, which is known to lead to code execution vulnerabilities. When unpickling, the serialized data could be manipulated to run arbitrary code. Instead, consider serializing the relevant data as JSON or a similar text-based serialization format.]
findings.tool_output: Rule: python.flask.security.insecure-deserialization.insecure-deserialization | Severity: ERROR | OWASP: [A08:2017 - Insecure Deserialization A08:2021 - Software and Data Integrity Failures A08:2025 - Software or Data Integrity Failures]

findings.title: python.lang.security.audit.subprocess-shell-true.subprocess-shell-true: Found 'subprocess' function 'run' with 'shell=True'. This is dangerous because this call will spawn the command using a shell process. Doing so propagates current shell settings and variables, which makes it much easier for a malicious actor to execute commands. Use 'shell=False' instead.
findings.description: Semgrep rule python.lang.security.audit.subprocess-shell-true.subprocess-shell-true detected: Found 'subprocess' function 'run' with 'shell=True'. This is dangerous because this call will spawn the command using a shell process. Doing so propagates current shell settings and variables, which makes it much easier for a malicious actor to execute commands. Use 'shell=False' instead.
findings.cwe: CWE-78: Improper Neutralization of Special Elements used in an OS Command ('OS Command Injection')
findings.tool_output: Rule: python.lang.security.audit.subprocess-shell-true.subprocess-shell-true | Severity: ERROR | OWASP: [A01:2017 - Injection A03:2021 - Injection A05:2025 - Injection]

These libraries are prone to code execution vulnerabilities. Ensure user data does not enter this function. To fix this, try to avoid serializing whole objects. Consider instead using a serializer such as JSON.
findings.description:
Semgrep rule python.flask.security.insecure-deserialization.insecure-deserialization detected: Detected the use of an insecure deserialization library in a Flask route. These libraries are prone to code execution vulnerabilities. Ensure user data does not enter this function. To fix this, try to avoid serializing whole objects. Consider instead using a serializer such as JSON.
[Also reported by another step: Semgrep rule python.lang.security.deserialization.avoid-pyyaml-load.avoid-pyyaml-load detected: Detected a possible YAML deserialization vulnerability. `yaml.unsafe_load`, `yaml.Loader`, `yaml.CLoader`, and `yaml.UnsafeLoader` are all known to be unsafe methods of deserializing YAML. An attacker with control over the YAML input could create special YAML input that allows the attacker to run arbitrary Python code. This would allow the attacker to steal files, download and install malware, or otherwise take over the machine. Use `yaml.safe_load` or `yaml.SafeLoader` instead.]
findings.tool_output: Rule: python.flask.security.insecure-deserialization.insecure-deserialization | Severity: ERROR | OWASP: [A08:2017 - Insecure Deserialization A08:2021 - Software and Data Integrity Failures A08:2025 - Software or Data Integrity Failures]

Instead  the Python documentation recommends using `defusedxml`.

findings.title: python.lang.security.audit.eval-detected.eval-detected: Detected the use of eval(). eval() can be dangerous if used to evaluate dynamic content. findings.cwe: CWE-95: Improper Neutralization of Directives in Dynamically Evaluated Code ('Eval Injection')
findings.tool_output: Rule: python.lang.security.audit.eval-detected.eval-detected | Severity: WARNING | OWASP: [A03:2021 - Injection A05:2025 - Injection]

findings.title: python.flask.security.insecure-deserialization.insecure-deserialization: Detected the use of an insecure deserialization library in a Flask route. These libraries are prone to code execution vulnerabilities. Ensure user data does not enter this function. To fix this, try to avoid serializing whole objects. Consider instead using a serializer such as JSON.
findings.description:
Semgrep rule python.flask.security.insecure-deserialization.insecure-deserialization detected: Detected the use of an insecure deserialization library in a Flask route. These libraries are prone to code execution vulnerabilities. Ensure user data does not enter this function. To fix this, try to avoid serializing whole objects. Consider instead using a serializer such as JSON.
[Also reported by another step: Semgrep rule python.lang.security.deserialization.pickle.avoid-pickle detected: Avoid using `pickle`, which is known to lead to code execution vulnerabilities. When unpickling, the serialized data could be manipulated to run arbitrary code. Instead, consider serializing the relevant data as JSON or a similar text-based serialization format.]
findings.tool_output: Rule: python.flask.security.insecure-deserialization.insecure-deserialization | Severity: ERROR | OWASP: [A08:2017 - Insecure Deserialization A08:2021 - Software and Data Integrity Failures A08:2025 - Software or Data Integrity Failures]

findings.title: python.lang.security.audit.subprocess-shell-true.subprocess-shell-true: Found 'subprocess' function 'run' with 'shell=True'. This is dangerous because this call will spawn the command using a shell process. Doing so propagates current shell settings and variables, which makes it much easier for a malicious actor to execute commands. Use 'shell=False' instead.
findings.description: Semgrep rule python.lang.security.audit.subprocess-shell-true.subprocess-shell-true detected: Found 'subprocess' function 'run' with 'shell=True'. This is dangerous because this call will spawn the command using a shell process. Doing so propagates current shell settings and variables, which makes it much easier for a malicious actor to execute commands. Use 'shell=False' instead.
findings.cwe: CWE-78: Improper Neutralization of Special Elements used in an OS Command ('OS Command Injection')
findings.tool_output: Rule: python.lang.security.audit.subprocess-shell-true.subprocess-shell-true | Severity: ERROR | OWASP: [A01:2017 - Injection A03:2021 - Injection A05:2025 - Injection]

findings.title: python.flask.security.audit.secure-set-cookie.secure-set-cookie: Found a Flask cookie with insecurely configured properties.  By default the secure, httponly and samesite ar configured insecurely. cookies should be handled securely by setting `secure=True`, `httponly=True`, and `samesite='Lax'` in response.set_cookie(...). If these parameters are not properly set, your cookies are not properly protected and are at risk of being stolen by an attacker. Include the `secure=True`, `httponly=True`, `samesite='Lax'` arguments or set these to be true in the Flask configuration.
findings.description: Semgrep rule python.flask.security.audit.secure-set-cookie.secure-set-cookie detected: Found a Flask cookie with insecurely configured properties.  By default the secure, httponly and samesite ar configured insecurely. cookies should be handled securely by setting `secure=True`, `httponly=True`, and `samesite='Lax'` in response.set_cookie(...). If these parameters are not properly set, your cookies are not properly protected and are at risk of being stolen by an attacker. Include the `secure=True`, `httponly=True`, `samesite='Lax'` arguments or set these to be true in the Flask configuration.
findings.cwe: CWE-614: Sensitive Cookie in HTTPS Session Without 'Secure' Attribute
findings.tool_output: Rule: python.flask.security.audit.secure-set-cookie.secure-set-cookie | Severity: WARNING | OWASP: [A05:2021 - Security Misconfiguration A02:2025 - Security Misconfiguration]

findings.title: python.flask.security.audit.xss.make-response-with-unknown-content.make-response-with-unknown-content: Be careful with `flask.make_response()`. If this response is rendered onto a webpage, this could create a cross-site scripting (XSS) vulnerability. `flask.make_response()` will not autoescape HTML. If you are rendering HTML, write your HTML in a template file and use `flask.render_template()` which will take care of escaping. If you are returning data from an API, consider using `flask.jsonify()`.
findings.description: Semgrep rule python.flask.security.audit.xss.make-response-with-unknown-content.make-response-with-unknown-content detected: Be careful with `flask.make_response()`. If this response is rendered onto a webpage, this could create a cross-site scripting (XSS) vulnerability. `flask.make_response()` will not autoescape HTML. If you are rendering HTML, write your HTML in a template file and use `flask.render_template()` which will take care of escaping. If you are returning data from an API, consider using `flask.jsonify()`.
findings.cwe: CWE-79: Improper Neutralization of Input During Web Page Generation ('Cross-site Scripting')
findings.tool_output: Rule: python.flask.security.audit.xss.make-response-with-unknown-content.make-response-with-unknown-content | Severity: WARNING | OWASP: [A07:2017 - Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) A03:2021 - Injection A05:2025 - Injection]

findings.title: python.flask.security.audit.xss.make-response-with-unknown-content.make-response-with-unknown-content: Be careful with `flask.make_response()`. If this response is rendered onto a webpage, this could create a cross-site scripting (XSS) vulnerability. `flask.make_response()` will not autoescape HTML. If you are rendering HTML, write your HTML in a template file and use `flask.render_template()` which will take care of escaping. If you are returning data from an API, consider using `flask.jsonify()`.
findings.description: Semgrep rule python.flask.security.audit.xss.make-response-with-unknown-content.make-response-with-unknown-content detected: Be careful with `flask.make_response()`. If this response is rendered onto a webpage, this could create a cross-site scripting (XSS) vulnerability. `flask.make_response()` will not autoescape HTML. If you are rendering HTML, write your HTML in a template file and use `flask.render_template()` which will take care of escaping. If you are returning data from an API, consider using `flask.jsonify()`.
findings.cwe: CWE-79: Improper Neutralization of Input During Web Page Generation ('Cross-site Scripting')
findings.tool_output: Rule: python.flask.security.audit.xss.make-response-with-unknown-content.make-response-with-unknown-content | Severity: WARNING | OWASP: [A07:2017 - Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) A03:2021 - Injection A05:2025 - Injection]

findings.title: python.flask.security.audit.secure-set-cookie.secure-set-cookie: Found a Flask cookie with insecurely configured properties.  By default the secure, httponly and samesite ar configured insecurely. cookies should be handled securely by setting `secure=True`, `httponly=True`, and `samesite='Lax'` in response.set_cookie(...). If these parameters are not properly set, your cookies are not properly protected and are at risk of being stolen by an attacker. Include the `secure=True`, `httponly=True`, `samesite='Lax'` arguments or set these to be true in the Flask configuration.
findings.description: Semgrep rule python.flask.security.audit.secure-set-cookie.secure-set-cookie detected: Found a Flask cookie with insecurely configured properties.  By default the secure, httponly and samesite ar configured insecurely. cookies should be handled securely by setting `secure=True`, `httponly=True`, and `samesite='Lax'` in response.set_cookie(...). If these parameters are not properly set, your cookies are not properly protected and are at risk of being stolen by an attacker. Include the `secure=True`, `httponly=True`, `samesite='Lax'` arguments or set these to be true in the Flask configuration.
findings.cwe: CWE-614: Sensitive Cookie in HTTPS Session Without 'Secure' Attribute
findings.tool_output: Rule: python.flask.security.audit.secure-set-cookie.secure-set-cookie | Severity: WARNING | OWASP: [A05:2021 - Security Misconfiguration A02:2025 - Security Misconfiguration]

findings.title: python.django.security.injection.raw-html-format.raw-html-format: Detected user input flowing into a manually constructed HTML string. You may be accidentally bypassing secure methods of rendering HTML by manually constructing HTML and this could create a cross-site scripting vulnerability, which could let attackers steal sensitive user data. To be sure this is safe, check that the HTML is rendered safely. Otherwise, use templates (`django.shortcuts.render`) which will safely render HTML instead.
